pages.title: Swedish Massage in Los Angeles, CA — RelaxTouch Studio
pages.meta_description: Premium Swedish massage in Los Angeles by licensed therapists. Long flowing strokes, full-body relaxation. 60 min from $230. Open 9 AM to midnight, daily.
pages.content_html:
Swedish massage is the most-requested style at our Los Angeles studio — and for good reason. Long, flowing strokes that work with your nervous system, not against it. The session most people walked into a spa picturing in their head.

This page covers what Swedish massage is, what to expect from a session at our LA location, who it's right for, and how it differs from the deeper modalities like deep tissue or hot stone.

What Swedish massage is

Swedish is the foundational technique most American massage therapists are licensed in. The strokes — long gliding pressure (effleurage), kneading (petrissage), and rhythmic tapping — work the surface and middle layers of muscle while signaling to your parasympathetic nervous system that it's safe to drop out of fight-or-flight.

Pressure is moderate. Firm enough that you feel real work happening; never so firm that it hurts. If you've ever had a session described as "a real massage, but pleasant" — that's Swedish.

What to expect at our LA studio

Sixty minutes is our most-booked length and the standard recommendation for first-time guests. Here's the rhythm:

  • 10 minutes intake + getting settled. We ask about pressure preferences, problem areas, anything to avoid.
  • 50-55 minutes on the table. Heated linens, premium organic oils, soft lighting, music curated for the room.
  • 2-3 minutes of stillness at the end. Most guests remember this part most.

The therapist works through the back, neck, shoulders, arms, hands, then has you turn over for the front body — legs, feet, decolletage. Standard Swedish covers the full body.

Who it's right for

  • First-time guests — gentle introduction to massage
  • Stress-driven tension — Swedish is the strongest parasympathetic reset modality
  • Sleep issues — sessions in the late afternoon often produce a notably better night
  • People recovering from illness, jet lag, or a heavy week — your body wants kindness, not aggressive deep work
  • Pregnancy — Swedish-style strokes adapted for prenatal positioning

Who might prefer something else

If you have chronic muscular tension in a specific area (the kind that's been there for months), pure Swedish often won't be enough. The right move is a hybrid session — mostly Swedish with 10-15 minutes of deep tissue work in your problem zone.

If you have injury recovery needs, sports-massage or deep tissue is the better starting point.

Tell us at intake what you're dealing with and we'll plan the session around it.

Pricing in Los Angeles

LengthPrice
60-minute Swedish$230
30-minute Swedish$200
15-minute reset$140

Booking

Same-day mid-day appointments are often available. Evenings and weekends — book a day or two ahead.

We're open 9 AM to midnight, every day. Late sessions, early sessions, between-meeting sessions — all at the same standard.

Book at our LA studio →

Frequently asked questions

How is Swedish massage different from a deep tissue massage?

Pressure and pace. Swedish is medium pressure with continuous flowing strokes — relaxing. Deep tissue uses firmer pressure and slower, more deliberate work — therapeutic. Different goals, different tools.

Should I book Swedish for my first massage in LA?

Yes. Swedish is the standard recommendation for first-time guests. If you find you want more depth on the second visit, you can switch then.

How often should I book Swedish in Los Angeles?

For stress management, every 2-3 weeks works well. For maintenance and general well-being, monthly. The compound effect of regular Swedish massage is more than people expect.

Will I be sore the next day?

Almost never with Swedish. It's the gentlest of the modalities. If you're sore after Swedish, the pressure was too firm — tell your therapist next time.

Do I need to undress fully?

You undress to your level of comfort. Many guests keep underwear on; many don't. Either is normal. You're under heated linens the whole time and only the area being worked on is uncovered.

pages.title: Deep Tissue Massage in Los Angeles, CA — RelaxTouch
pages.h1: Deep Tissue Massage in Los Angeles
pages.meta_description: Deep tissue massage in Los Angeles by licensed therapists. Targets chronic tension in shoulders, back, hips. 60 min from $230. Open 9 AM to midnight, daily.
pages.content_html:
Deep tissue is the second most-requested modality at our Los Angeles studio. If you have chronic tension that hasn't responded to stretching, foam rolling, or shorter spa sessions — this is the work that often shifts it.

What deep tissue actually is

Deep tissue is targeted, slower-paced massage with firmer pressure. The therapist works specific muscle groups — often perpendicular to the muscle fibers — to release adhesions, lengthen chronically shortened tissue, and break up patterns that have been there for months.

It's not about pain. A skilled therapist works at the edge of comfortable pressure, never past it. If you're holding your breath, the pressure is too much. The deepest, most lasting work happens at your edge — not over it.

What our LA clients book it for

  • Desk-job upper back and shoulders — the most common request from LA tech and entertainment professionals
  • Lower back tension from long commutes / driving / hours behind a wheel on the 405
  • Hip flexor tightness from sitting + workout-cycle imbalances
  • IT band area for runners, hikers, cyclists
  • Neck pain from screen time — forward-head posture creates very specific patterns

What to expect from your first deep tissue session

Mild soreness for 24-48 hours after is normal — like the day after a new workout. This is the muscle responding to the work, not a sign anything went wrong. Hydrate, take a warm bath if you can.

Most people notice clear differences in their problem area for 5-10 days after the first session. By the third session, the pattern often shifts more permanently.

How often to book

For active chronic pain — weekly to bi-weekly for the first 3-4 sessions, then taper to bi-weekly or monthly maintenance.

For maintenance after the issue has resolved — every 3-4 weeks. Skip and the pattern often returns within 4-6 weeks.

Pricing

LengthPrice
60-minute deep tissue$230
30-minute focused work$200

Most guests booking for chronic tension start with 60 minutes — gives the therapist time to warm up the area before going deep.

The hybrid most people actually want

If you're not sure whether to book Swedish or deep tissue, the honest middle is the most-useful session for most adults: 60 minutes Swedish with 15-20 minutes of deep tissue focus on your problem area. Tell us when you book and the therapist plans the session.

Book deep tissue at our LA studio →

Frequently asked questions

Is deep tissue painful?

It can be intense in chronically tight areas — never painful. A skilled therapist works at your edge, not past it. If anything ever hurts, say so.

How is it different from Swedish massage?

Pressure, pace, intent. Swedish relaxes; deep tissue treats. Different goals.

Should I get deep tissue if I'm sore from a workout?

Wait 24-48 hours after a hard workout before deep tissue — your muscles are already in a stressed state. Light Swedish is fine immediately after.

How long until I see improvement in my chronic tension?

Usually 3-6 sessions for established patterns. Don't expect a single session to fix months of accumulated tension.

Can I get deep tissue while pregnant?

Skip deep tissue during pregnancy. Book a prenatal-certified therapist for prenatal-specific work.

pages.title: Hot Stone Massage in Los Angeles, CA — RelaxTouch
pages.h1: Hot Stone Massage in Los Angeles
pages.meta_description: Hot stone massage in Los Angeles. Heated basalt stones, deeper relaxation than Swedish, gentler than deep tissue. 60 min from $230. Open 9 AM to midnight.
pages.content_html:
Hot stone is one of the most under-appreciated modalities — gimmick-sounding, genuinely effective. Heated basalt stones reach a deeper layer of muscle than hands alone in a fraction of the warm-up time, and the parasympathetic effect of sustained warmth is harder to resist than any other massage we offer.

How it works

Smooth volcanic stones (basalt — chosen for its density and heat retention) are warmed in water to 120-130°F and used in two ways:

  • As glide tools — gentle, sustained pressure along muscle groups while transferring heat into the tissue
  • As pressure points — placed on tight areas for a few minutes while the therapist works elsewhere; the warmth softens the layer for later work

Heat dilates blood vessels, increases local circulation, and reduces muscle viscosity. Translation: tissue becomes more pliable in 3 minutes than 10 minutes of hands-only warm-up would achieve.

Best for

  • People who run cold — hot stone goes deeper than just "feels nice"
  • LA winters and cool nights — December through February is peak hot stone season here
  • Chronic muscle tension that resists deep tissue — sometimes heat does what additional pressure can't
  • Anyone who has trouble relaxing — sustained warmth is the strongest parasympathetic signal there is
  • Frequent flyers — long flights leave bodies cold and stiff. Hot stone resets that

Skip hot stone if you have

Pregnancy, diabetes with neuropathy, cardiovascular conditions limiting heat exposure, recent inflammatory injuries, fever, sunburn, or open wounds in the area being worked. Tell us at intake — we'll either modify or recommend a different session.

Booking

Hot stone sessions are 60 or 90 minutes. The 90-minute version is what regulars choose during winter — it's the most thorough deep relaxation we offer.

LengthPrice
60 minutes$230
90 minutes$320

Book hot stone at our LA studio →

Frequently asked questions

Are the stones safe?

Yes. Therapists test each stone on their forearm before placing it on you. Temperature is monitored throughout. If anything ever feels too hot, tell us — they're swapped instantly.

Will I feel the heat through the linens?

Yes — and that's the point. The heat is what makes hot stone effective.

How does it compare to a sauna?

Different pathway. Sauna heats the surface from the outside via air. Hot stone heats specific muscles from contact. Both relax you; hot stone reaches problem tissue more directly.

Is it worth the upgrade over Swedish?

For winter sessions and people who run cold — usually yes. For year-round, depends on your preference. Many regulars rotate hot stone in winter and Swedish/deep tissue the rest of the year.

Will the heat dehydrate me?

Slightly more than a regular massage. Drink an extra glass of water on the way home.

pages.title: Sports Recovery Massage in Los Angeles, CA — RelaxTouch
pages.h1: Sports Recovery Massage in Los Angeles
pages.meta_description: Sports massage for runners, cyclists, gym athletes in Los Angeles. Targeted recovery work for overused muscle groups. 60 min from $230. Open 9 AM to midnight.
pages.content_html:
LA is one of the most active cities in the country — Runyon Canyon at 6 AM, beach runs, cycling Mulholland, high-intensity gym scenes. Sports recovery massage is targeted bodywork for the body that has to keep showing up to all of it.

What sports massage actually addresses

Sports massage isn't one technique — it's a category combining deep tissue, trigger-point work, myofascial release, and assisted stretching, delivered by a therapist who understands movement patterns and recovery cycles.

Common LA-specific overuse patterns we treat:

  • Runners — calves, hamstrings, IT band area, glute med
  • Cyclists — hip flexors, quads, lower back
  • Gym athletes — lats, traps, rotator cuffs
  • Pickleball / tennis — forearms, shoulders, hips
  • Yogis — wrists, neck, low back from heavy practice

How to time it around training

Maintenance massage: every 2-3 weeks during regular training, weekly during high-volume blocks. Moderately deep work; mild soreness OK.

Pre-race: 2-3 days before a race, only light Swedish. Skip deep tissue in the 72 hours before — it can leave you sore on race day.

Post-race: 24-48 hours after, light work. Wait 5-7 days before deep tissue.

Booking

Tell your therapist your weekly mileage, your next hard event, current niggles, and your target. The session changes based on this information.

Book sports massage at our LA studio →

Frequently asked questions

How does sports massage differ from deep tissue?

Sports massage is deep tissue tuned for athletes — same depth, different focus. Movement-restoration emphasis vs. just releasing tension.

How long before a marathon should my last deep tissue session be?

5-7 days minimum. Some elite athletes go 10. Light Swedish 2-3 days out is fine.

Should I get massaged before a tough hike?

Light Swedish 1-2 days before is fine. Skip deep tissue same-day or day-before.

I'm sore the day after — what does that mean?

Normal — like the day after a new workout. Hydrate, walk, gentle stretch. Soreness should pass in 48 hours.

Do I need to share my training schedule with the therapist?

Yes. Weekly mileage, recent hard sessions, target event date — all of it shapes the session.

pages.title: Prenatal Massage in Los Angeles, CA — RelaxTouch
pages.meta_description: Prenatal massage in Los Angeles by certified therapists. Side-lying position, lower back + hip relief, third-trimester sleep support. From $230. Open 9 AM-12 AM.
pages.content_html:
Pregnancy reshapes the body weekly. Prenatal massage at our Los Angeles studio is performed only by therapists certified in prenatal work — the positioning, modifications, and contraindications are different from regular massage, and your safety and your baby's are the priorities.

When to book

Most clients start in their second trimester (week 14+). The first trimester we generally decline at our studio — not because massage causes problems, but because most miscarriages happen in those weeks for unrelated reasons and the timing creates an unhelpful association.

If you want first-trimester massage with OB clearance, talk to us — we may be able to accommodate with light Swedish in supported side-lying.

What's different about prenatal sessions

  • Side-lying position with bolsters supporting the right knee, between arms, head. After ~20 weeks supine (back) is avoided for extended periods.
  • Focus areas: lower back, hips, glutes — where the most discomfort lives in pregnancy.
  • Avoided: deep abdominal work, deep pressure on certain points historically associated with labor (evidence weak but conservative practice).
  • Pressure: moderate. No deep tissue during pregnancy.
  • Aromatherapy: pregnancy-safe oils only or fragrance-free. Tell us at intake.

Trimester-by-trimester at our LA studio

Second trimester (weeks 14-27): The sweet spot. Most regulars come every 2-3 weeks. Lower back pain prevention becomes the dominant request.

Third trimester (weeks 28-40): Often weekly. Sciatic-pattern pain, edema in legs and feet, and sleep support. The single best night of sleep most clients get all week often follows a third-trimester session.

Postnatal: From 6 weeks postpartum (after standard OB clearance), sometimes earlier with provider OK. Different work — recovery from labor, breastfeeding posture strain, scar-tissue work for C-sections.

When NOT to book without OB clearance

  • High-risk pregnancy designation
  • History of preterm labor
  • Severe pre-eclampsia / pregnancy-induced hypertension
  • Placenta previa
  • Severe edema or sudden swelling
  • Recent vaginal bleeding
  • DVT history
  • Severe gestational diabetes
  • Multiples (twins+) — coordinate with OB

None of these are absolute prohibitions — they require medical coordination.

Pricing

LengthPrice
60-minute prenatal$230
90-minute prenatal$320

Book prenatal at our LA studio →

Frequently asked questions

Is prenatal massage safe?

For most uncomplicated pregnancies after the first trimester — yes, when performed by a prenatal-certified therapist. Coordinate with your OB if you have specific medical conditions.

Can I lie on my back?

Briefly, yes. For extended supine after ~20 weeks, no — the uterus can compress the inferior vena cava and reduce blood flow. We use side-lying primarily.

Will massage induce labor?

The evidence is weak that any standard technique induces labor. Trained prenatal therapists know the few specific points to avoid in late pregnancy.

Can my partner come?

Most studios allow a partner in the lobby. Some larger rooms accommodate a partner sitting nearby. Ask when booking.

How is prenatal priced?

Same as regular sessions of the same length. No upcharge.

pages.title: Relaxation Massage in Los Angeles, CA — RelaxTouch Studio
pages.meta_description: Pure relaxation massage in Los Angeles. Gentle, full-body, nervous system reset. No focused deep work. 60 min from $230. Open 9 AM to midnight, daily.
pages.content_html:
Sometimes you don't have a problem area. Sometimes you don't want the therapist to "work on something." Sometimes you just need an hour where the world stops. That's relaxation massage.

What it is

Relaxation massage is the gentlest version of Swedish — slow, continuous strokes at a light-to-medium pressure, performed across the full body without focused therapeutic work on any specific area. The goal is single: parasympathetic nervous-system reset.

What it isn't

  • Not deep tissue — pressure stays light
  • Not problem-focused — therapist doesn't dig into knots
  • Not a massage where you'll be sore tomorrow
  • Not what you book if you have a specific injury or pain

Who it's right for

  • Stress so deep that even Swedish feels intense
  • Recovery from illness, jet lag, a brutal week
  • First-time clients who are nervous about "real" massage
  • Anyone with sensitive skin / low pressure tolerance
  • People who just want to fall asleep on the table — it's encouraged

Pricing

LengthPrice
60-minute relaxation$230
90-minute relaxation$320

For pure stress relief, the 90-minute version is what regulars settle into. The first 30 minutes get you out of fight-or-flight. The next 60 minutes is where the real reset happens.

Book relaxation at our LA studio →

Frequently asked questions

What's the difference between Swedish and relaxation massage?

Mostly intent. Swedish is broader — can include therapeutic focus on tight areas. Relaxation is purely calming with no focused work. Pressure is also slightly lighter in relaxation.

Will I fall asleep?

Probably yes. Many people do. Therapists continue working — sleep is a sign the session is working.

Is this good for my first-ever massage?

Excellent choice. No pressure surprises, no soreness afterward, just a calm hour.

Will I get any therapeutic benefit?

Stress reduction is therapeutic. Lower cortisol, better sleep, lower blood pressure, calmer nervous system. The science is real even when the work is gentle.

How often should I book?

For chronic stress — weekly to bi-weekly, indefinitely. The compound effect builds over months.
